summary: Do not ship B-1 yet: S-1’s overwrite/resurrection race is closed, but the repair introduces an unbounded per-key liveness failure without an accepted recovery contract.

findings.title: Uncancellable KeyGuard ownership can wedge a key indefinitely
findings.body: `commit_pointer` transfers `KeyGuard` into an abort-resistant blocking closure and releases it only after `commit_blocking` returns. Under the plan’s explicitly modeled non-returning filesystem operation, an upload timeout only drops the caller future; the detached closure retains the key until the syscall returns or the process is killed. Same-key DELETE has no timeout (`src/store/objects.rs:158`), while PUT retries reserve global capacity before waiting (`src/http/internal/files.rs:82-92`), potentially spreading the outage. T-S1 confirms blocking only until its manual release and does not cover this degraded path. This is a new availability tradeoff introduced by the S-1 repair.
findings.recommendation: Do not simply release the guard on timeout, which would reopen S-1. Before B-2, either implement key-bound fencing/versioned pointer publication so newer operations can supersede a detached commit without stale resurrection, or explicitly escalate and accept the restart-required recovery contract with observability and operational limits. Add a deterministic parked-commit test proving the chosen bounded behavior.

next_steps: Escalate this availability tradeoff because it exceeds the standing approval for test-only choreography changes.
Define and pin the same-key recovery contract before building B-2 on this commit seam.
hint: Branch review only sees committed content (base...HEAD), but the working tree has uncommitted changes that were NOT reviewed. Commit (or stash) them, then re-run — otherwise unreviewed edits pass the gate. If the dirt is a prior gate's docs/reviews/*/<gate>-r*.json artifact, COMMIT it (don't stash): the conductors' artifact-derived done checks read the committed file.
